chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: This is the story of how I used comics to help me in my role  as a caregiver for my mom,  in a way I couldn't have imagined.  Now, when I was thinking about how to tell that story,  I talked a lot about it with my mom.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: That's because this story is hers.  My mom's name is Jocelyn, and we're not alike.  She's an optimist.  I worry about all the terrible things that are going to go wrong.  She's impulsive, and she's a little bit of a wimp.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: We're so different that it took me a long time to realize  we had something important in common.  We both respond to challenges by writing stories.  Now, it took me such a long time to notice that we had this in common  because the stories we wrote were so different.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She self-published a book of poems and short stories  about living with Parkinson's disease over the last 20 years.  I write comics, like this one.  And my comics are about stuff like this.  Now, over the years, my mom and I got used to her physical health challenges.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But a while ago, she started having cognitive challenges too.  She couldn't write stories anymore,  and communication between us kept getting harder.  My mom's doctor didn't get it.  He asked her questions like,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Well, what city are you in?  She knows the answer.  He asked, What year is it?  Well, she knew that answer too.  He just said, You're fine.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But my mom's mom was a little bit more of a wimp.  She said, You're fine.  But my mom had been having hallucinations.  She sometimes thought she was surrounded by ghostly people.  The doctor couldn't see it.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But my mom could.  And now, so can you.  I want to tell you about graphic medicine,  a growing movement that refers to an unlikely partnership  between health care and comics.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now, at the heart of this movement is a kind of a comic  called a graphic pathography.  Graphic pathography just means a story about illness  that's told in a visual medium.  This comic is that kind of a story.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: You can see the ghostly hallucinations.  And you're invited to feel empathy  for the patient's experience.  You can share my mom's concern  that her symptoms have not been recognized.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: That's one way that words and pictures can work together  to tell a health care story.  And this is something anyone can do.  Now, you might be thinking,  No, I'm not a comics artist.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But that's OK.  You don't have to be.  A health care story can benefit from very simple pictures.  I'm going to show you how,  but we've got to go back a few years

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: to a time before I drew those pictures that I just shared.  My mom's condition got worse,  and we were in the hospital a lot.  By this time, hallucinations and early-stage dementia  made it hard for her to explain what was going on,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: and it was hard for the hospital staff to trust her.  I was constantly hanging around the hospital.  I felt like everything depended on me.  Doctors and nurses came and went,  arriving and leaving unexpectedly,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: and I felt like I was standing at the side of a highway  trying to get the attention of the drivers racing past.  There was this one evening  when I really had to get home to my kids,  but I didn't want to leave the hospital

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: because my mom had been having this weird symptom.  She was leaning off to the left.  Her head and upper body slumped sideways.  During the day, she'd slide out of her wheelchair  and her leg would get caught in the wheels.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And at night, her inability to straighten out her body  made it hard for her to get a good sleep.  Now, the doctor was aware of this,  and we'd ruled out the possibility  that there was anything dangerous going on like a stroke.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But still, I didn't want to leave  because the night staff were going to arrive,  and they didn't know my mom.  This sideways posture didn't have anything to do  with the reason she'd actually been admitted to the hospital.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: They might just overlook it.  Or if they did notice it,  they might assume this was just her regular posture,  and it really wasn't.  I didn't know what to do.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And that's when it came to me.  A picture could help.  So I drew one.  I wrote,  Help for Jocelyn.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She leans to the left.  Please support wheelchair and bed with pillows.  I drew a circle around the leg that kept getting injured.  And I drew my mom lying in bed.  And I wrote,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: This is a comfy sleeping position.  I taped it up on the wall above her bed,  and I left.  And suddenly I felt like  I didn't need to keep standing at the side of that highway.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: It was as if I just planted a big sign  at the side of the road that anyone passing by would see.  And I could go home.  Thanks to that picture, I got a good night's sleep.  And so did my mom.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: When I went back the next morning,  I saw that someone had propped up her left arm with a pillow.  A nurse who I'd never met had seen the picture  and known what to do.  This was the first of so many pictures I drew to help my mom,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: and what surprised me was how fast this worked.  I carried pictures like this around with me everywhere I went  to pull out whenever I needed them  to save me explaining things again.  And I learned that a picture is worth a thousand words

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: that you just don't have time to say.  Then my mom was moved to another part of the hospital,  and there was a whole new team of staff members  who didn't know her.  So I got ready to start drawing some new pictures.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: But then I thought,  when I drew those first pictures for my mom,  I had made choices about what health care issues  to highlight on her behalf.  At that time, she hadn't had the words to speak for herself,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: so those pictures were just my best guesses  about what might help.  But big questions arise  when you try to tell someone else's story.  That kind of collaboration depends on trust.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: So this time, I sat down with my mom  and I asked her what pictures I should draw.  Her answers surprised me.  She said,  Please, tell them to call me Jocelyn.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: They don't know I go by my middle name.  She said,  Please, tell them I'm left-handed.  And she asked me to draw a food tray  on which the items had been placed

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: where her hand could reach them.  She asked me to draw a picture that said,  Please remove lids.  That's because nerve damage in her hands  makes fine motor skills a challenge.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: She asked me to draw a picture that said,  Please fill cups halfway.  A full cup is too heavy.  She asked me to draw a picture that said,  Please tell me your name.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I can't read your name tag.  And she asked me to draw a picture  to go on the door of her room  so she would know which room was hers.  These small details were a big deal.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: They gave me insight into challenges  I hadn't even been aware of.  Now on the picture of the door of my mom's room,  I drew her face.  I've drawn my mom so many times,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: I have a way of drawing her face.  But the point isn't that it has to look  anything like my mom.  It could be a circle with two dots for eyes.  The point is that there's a face.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: There's a person with a voice.  And if you listen to the picture,  the voice can be heard  because the face can be seen.  The message matters more

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: because it comes from someone.  After I'd drawn all those pictures,  my mom asked me,  Now draw one more.  Draw me looking healthy.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Draw me walking with my walker  and label it Jocelyn's goal.  She said,  The staff here are just going to see  someone who's weak and confused.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: It's easy to think that's all I am.  She said,  I want them to understand  what we're working for.  Sometimes you have to see it to believe it.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: If I'm honest,  I have to admit that sometimes I was the one  who just saw the sick old lady in the hospital bed.  And trying to capture my mom's goal in a picture  helped me believe in it more myself.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: My mom did reach her goal,  and a few months later,  she walked out of the hospital  on her own two feet.  She moved to long-term care,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: and for the first time,  her care needs were more managed and predictable.  Now, she did still hallucinate  about being surrounded by ghostly people.  But my mom and I have always responded to challenges

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: by writing stories.  And now, we've learned to write stories together.  Like this one.  Here's me asking my mom,  How's the writing going?

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And she responds,  Not great.  Maybe I need a ghostwriter.  I already have the ghost.  Remember my mom's doctor,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: the one who didn't get it?  That comic about him was part of this same story.  It's a comic my mom and I wrote together  for a magazine dedicated to destigmatizing dementia  and supporting people impacted by this disease.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: My mom's name appeared in the byline  right next to mine.  And this comic was one of the ways  we carefully documented her symptoms,  which led to her being able to start a new medication

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: that helped with those ghostly hallucinations.  But more than that,  it was a comic that was written by my mom  to help people with their own hallucinations.  But more than that,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: this comic let her use her experience  to help others whom the magazine could reach.  And besides, isn't it just cool  that a medical magazine these days has comics?  My mom and I have continued to write comics together,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: and she's continued to trust me  with sharing the stories of life with dementia  and life in long-term care during the pandemic.  I think she's been okay with me  sharing these vulnerable moments

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: because she knows I'm not just telling the story  of a sick old lady in the hospital bed.  She knows I understand  that even though I may be the one drawing the pictures,  she's a collaborator with an equal part in the work.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: And here's my mom saying,  do not write about that in this comic.  The reason this all started  didn't have anything to do with art or writing  or even healthcare.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: It came from me wanting to help my mom.  And that's the same power you have in your relationships  with the people you care for.  You know their healthcare needs.  You live their stories with them.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Now, I understand you may still feel a bit skeptical  about showing up at the doctor's office with a sketchbook,  but you may be surprised to discover  that the people in your healthcare community  are already familiar with graphic medicine,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: the growing movement  at the intersection of healthcare and comics.  They may already know how a picture can be  an amazing time saver  or a tool for creating empathy and personal connections.

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: Just imagine if your new doctor opened your chart  and saw pictures that sparked curiosity  about the person, not just the symptoms.  When I looked at all the pictures I'd drawn of my mom,  I did see her symptoms,

chunks.title: How_Comic_Strips_Create_Better_Health_Care
chunks.text: but I also see my mom.  She's there in all the words and pictures  that have continued to hold us together.
